name: executioner ceremonial axe
shortDescription: An executioner ceremonial axe polished beyond ordinary use, meant for grim court rituals, oath halls, and official displays where authority needs a sharp silhouette. It is handled more like regalia than equipment, though no wise merchant forgets what shape the regalia takes.
flavorText: “Some ceremonies are remembered because everyone speaks softly.”

name: massive executioner axe
shortDescription: A massive executioner axe with a broad head, long haft, and enough weight to make even strong buyers test their grip twice. Its market is narrow but intense: magistrates, collectors, theaters, and fortress stores that prefer symbols too large to misplace.
flavorText: “A tool this large does not ask to be understood.”

name: fancy executioner axe
shortDescription: A fancy executioner axe with bright fittings, formal engraving, and a finish that makes its purpose look uncomfortably expensive. Nobles buy it for grim pageantry, museums buy it for locked cases, and traveling performers buy convincing copies when audiences pay well for dread.
flavorText: “Decoration cannot soften an edge, but it can make people stare longer.”

name: ironclan executioner axe
shortDescription: An ironclan executioner axe forged in the heavy, bluntly practical style of mountain halls and old clan law. The metalwork is severe, the haft is reinforced, and the whole piece carries the uncomfortable confidence of a tradition that expects obedience.
flavorText: “Ironclan craft rarely wastes metal on apologies.”

name: strong ironclan executioner axe
shortDescription: A strong ironclan executioner axe built with extra weight at the head, thick socket bands, and a haft meant to survive generations of ceremonial handling. It is less ornate than costly court pieces, but its durability gives it the colder prestige of old stone law.
flavorText: “The clans call it strong because they expect history to pull on it.”

name: small hand axe
shortDescription: A small hand axe suited to camp chores, brush clearing, emergency wagon repair, and travelers who want a tool that fits beside rope and tinder. It is not impressive in a hall, but on a wet road it earns its keep before breakfast.
flavorText: “The humble axe is usually the first one unpacked.”

name: large hand axe
shortDescription: A large hand axe with more reach and bite than a camp hatchet, favored by caravan guards, foresters, and settlers who need one tool to handle wood, stakes, crates, and trouble. Its size makes it useful, but also slightly too obvious to call casual.
flavorText: “A hand axe grows large when the road grows uncertain.”

name: strong hand axe
shortDescription: A strong hand axe with reinforced haft, firm grip, and a compact head built for hard daily work. It is prized by people who break tools too often: muleteers, lumber hands, roofers, and anyone whose profession begins with stubborn material.
flavorText: “Strength matters most in the tools nobody sings about.”

name: sharpened hand axe
shortDescription: A sharpened hand axe honed to a clean edge and ready for close cutting, camp work, or careful utility. Buyers test the bite on scrap wood, then usually ask for a sheath, because a tool this eager should not share a sack with blankets.
flavorText: “A sharp hand axe makes small work feel decisive.”

name: fancy hand axe
shortDescription: A fancy hand axe with decorated grip, polished metal, and just enough ornament to make a practical buyer pretend it is still purely practical. It sells well as a gift to scouts, nobles with hunting lodges, and merchants who like tools that can be shown off.
flavorText: “A useful thing becomes a present when someone engraves the handle.”

name: freshly bloody battle axe
shortDescription: A freshly bloody battle axe with recent red staining that makes most civil buyers step back and ask fewer questions. Salvagers, quartermasters, alchemists, and grim collectors value it differently, but every honest shopkeeper wraps it before displaying it.
flavorText: “Fresh marks turn a weapon into evidence.”

name: battle axe covered in dried blood
shortDescription: A battle axe covered in dried blood, darkened around the head and haft after poor cleaning or deliberate display. Some buyers see battlefield history, others see bad maintenance, and experienced merchants price it only after deciding which story is easier to sell.
flavorText: “Old stains are cheaper than legends unless someone believes them.”

name: fabled axe of sleep
shortDescription: The fabled axe of sleep carries a drowsy enchantment in its runes, making it prized by collectors, wardens, and oddly practical duelists who prefer endings quieter than ordinary steel allows. It must be stored away from apprentices, cats, and anyone already leaning on the counter.
flavorText: “Some weapons promise rest. That does not make them gentle.”

name: fabled axe of woe
shortDescription: The fabled axe of woe is wrapped in old sorrowful charmwork, its edge said to carry the weight of bad news before it is even drawn. Buyers include curse scholars, tragic nobles, and collectors who believe every armory needs one object nobody enjoys touching.
flavorText: “Woe is difficult to appraise, but it keeps very well.”

name: axe of firestarting
shortDescription: An axe of firestarting enchanted to throw sparks from more than ordinary steel, useful to woodsmen, scouts, siege crews, and cooks with dangerous confidence. It sells with strict storage advice, because no warehouse wants a tool that can begin arguments with kindling.
flavorText: “Every camp loves it until the dry season.”

name: lumberjack axe
shortDescription: A lumberjack axe shaped for rhythm, reach, and the honest misery of hard timber. Foresters judge its balance with a practiced swing, while merchants know it sells best in frontier towns where roofs, fences, fires, and wagons all begin as trees.
flavorText: “A lumberjack axe turns forests into invoices.”

name: small war axe
shortDescription: A small war axe made for close fighting, fast movement, and soldiers who value a weapon that does not fight the belt. It is compact enough for patrols, sharp enough for military buyers, and plain enough for quartermasters to order without ceremony.
flavorText: “Small weapons survive by being where they are needed.”

name: small war axe with laced gold
shortDescription: A small war axe with laced gold worked into its fittings, blending battlefield practicality with the vanity of rank. Officers, champions, and noble guards buy it when they want compact authority that glints before anyone asks who is in command.
flavorText: “Gold does not improve the swing, only the entrance.”

name: war axe
shortDescription: A war axe of dependable campaign make, heavier than a hand tool and meaner than a woodsman’s blade. It belongs in militia racks, fortress stores, escort wagons, and any market close enough to border trouble for steel to sell faster than furniture.
flavorText: “A war axe is not subtle, but neither are war prices.”

name: strong war axe
shortDescription: A strong war axe with reinforced head, sturdy haft, and balance intended for long service under rough hands. It costs more than ordinary stock, but captains understand that a weapon which survives three campaigns is cheaper than three replacements.
flavorText: “Strong arms help. Strong sockets matter longer.”

name: notable starcourt bow
shortDescription: A notable starcourt bow decorated with pale celestial motifs and courtly workmanship, prized by archers who want grace without losing pull strength. Its value comes from both performance and rumor, since anything named Starcourt attracts patrons who enjoy sounding informed.
flavorText: “Stars are distant, but their branding travels well.”

name: notable vine bow
shortDescription: A notable vine bow carved with curling woodwork and fitted for smooth draw, favored by wardens, forest scouts, and noble hunters pretending to be humble. The limbs look grown rather than made, which makes buyers argue over whether it is art or equipment.
flavorText: “A vine bow bends like it learned patience from trees.”

name: fabled bow of light
shortDescription: The fabled bow of light carries a bright enchantment through polished limbs and clear string, sought by temple archers, monster hunters, and collectors who prefer their miracles with range. It is expensive, conspicuous, and nearly impossible to smuggle discreetly at dusk.
flavorText: “Light travels quickly. In a bow, it invoices slowly.”

name: market longbow
shortDescription: A market longbow of ordinary make, reliable enough for hunters, watchmen, and village militias without the price of noble craftsmanship. It draws cleanly, stores poorly in damp corners, and sells steadily wherever distance is considered a form of caution.
flavorText: “The market longbow is famous only for being available.”

name: accurate longbow
shortDescription: An accurate longbow with careful tiller, steady limbs, and a reputation for sending arrows where the archer intended rather than where excuses begin. Scouts and competition archers pay more for its consistency, especially when pride is scored in public.
flavorText: “Accuracy is the luxury that looks like discipline.”

name: powerful longbow
shortDescription: A powerful longbow with heavy draw and deep-storing force, meant for trained arms and buyers who understand that strength has a cost. It is excellent in capable hands and embarrassing in weak ones, which makes demonstrations unusually entertaining.
flavorText: “A powerful bow sells strength and reveals honesty.”

name: fabled bow of the moon
shortDescription: The fabled bow of the moon bears silvered markings and a cool enchantment that seems brighter under night skies. Moon priests, night scouts, and collectors of elegant danger prize it, though merchants mostly prize how easily the name raises the price.
flavorText: “Moonlight is free until someone fits it to a bow.”

name: scarce red bow
shortDescription: A scarce red bow dyed or lacquered in a deep striking finish, uncommon enough to catch attention before its draw is tested. It sells to duel archers, festival guards, and wealthy hunters who dislike equipment that disappears against ordinary walls.
flavorText: “A red bow announces the archer before the arrow does.”

name: royal shortbow
shortDescription: A royal shortbow with refined shaping, rich fittings, and the compact authority of palace sport turned serious. It is easy to carry, pleasant to display, and beloved by buyers who want a weapon that suggests access to better doors.
flavorText: “Royal weapons are often small because everyone else makes room.”

name: market shortbow
shortDescription: A market shortbow made for simple hunting, travel defense, and buyers who need something smaller than a longbow but more useful than optimism. It is common, affordable, and quick to move in frontier stalls where compact gear earns shelf space.
flavorText: “A shortbow fits where a longbow argues.”

name: strong longbow
shortDescription: A strong longbow with sturdy limbs and a dependable draw, built for archers who want power without the temperament of rare enchanted wood. It is a serious working bow, the kind purchased after someone has already outgrown cheaper mistakes.
flavorText: “A strong longbow does not flatter the unpracticed.”

name: strong shortbow
shortDescription: A strong shortbow with compact limbs, firm pull, and better force than its size first suggests. Caravan riders, scouts, and hunters value it because cramped roads and saddle movement reward weapons that do not need a large stage.
flavorText: “Small frames can still carry serious tension.”

name: elaborate bow
shortDescription: An elaborate bow carved with decorative lines, fitted accents, and enough craftsmanship to make even practical archers pause. It is bought for ceremonial hunts, court contests, and collections where beauty must still be able to bend correctly.
flavorText: “Ornament is safest when the bow still shoots straight.”

name: fancy tribal bow
shortDescription: A fancy tribal bow marked with beadwork, carved signs, and colored wraps tied to a specific maker’s tradition. Traders handle it carefully, since its value depends as much on cultural meaning and provenance as on range, draw, and polished finish.
flavorText: “A bow can carry a people’s handprint without saying a word.”

name: fabled bow of the sun
shortDescription: The fabled bow of the sun glows with warm gold charmwork and a reputation for bright, decisive shots. Temple champions, desert scouts, and noble collectors compete for it, though merchants mostly remember to keep it away from wax seals.
flavorText: “Sunlight becomes expensive when it learns to aim.”

name: scarce fruitwood bow
shortDescription: A scarce fruitwood bow shaped from fragrant, resilient timber whose grain gives it both charm and lively response. It attracts hunters, orchard clans, and wealthy hobbyists who like the idea of sweetness hidden inside a serious draw.
flavorText: “Fruitwood remembers blossoms, but bends for arrows.”

name: fabled bow of Long Dusk
shortDescription: The fabled bow of Long Dusk is said to hold the dim hour between daylight and danger in its polished limbs. It appeals to night wardens, border rangers, and collectors who prefer legends with shadows rather than clean daylight explanations.
flavorText: “Dusk lasts longer when a bow refuses to let it end.”

name: scarce bow of peircing
shortDescription: A scarce bow of peircing known for driving arrows with hard, narrow intent despite the maker’s famously misspelled inscription. Archers forgive the lettering if the shot lands true, while merchants quietly avoid correcting a name that already sells.
flavorText: “A misspelled legend can still puncture a shield.”

name: scarce bow of storms
shortDescription: A scarce bow of storms with darkened limbs, charged fittings, and a faint crackle that makes dry-haired buyers nervous. Storm hunters, ship guards, and weather-mages prize it for its temper, though careful shops never store it near loose paper.
flavorText: “Thunder is easier to hear than to warranty.”

name: scarce cherrywood bow
shortDescription: A scarce cherrywood bow with warm red grain, smooth polish, and an elegant spring that makes it popular with hunters of refined taste. Its beauty raises the price before the draw is tested, but its feel usually wins the argument afterward.
flavorText: “Cherrywood turns patience into color.”

name: ceremonial club
shortDescription: A ceremonial club carved and polished for rites, leadership signs, and formal guards rather than tavern brawls. It carries authority through shape and handling, making it popular with clans, shrine attendants, and collectors of objects that speak before being raised.
flavorText: “Ceremony often begins with something heavy enough to be respected.”

name: forked club
shortDescription: A forked club with a split striking head that gives it a rough, memorable profile and a nasty practicality. Marsh fighters, monster handlers, and stage warriors favor it because the shape suggests control as much as impact.
flavorText: “A forked club offers two opinions at once.”

name: giant spiked club
shortDescription: A giant spiked club built more like a portable threat than a refined weapon, too large for most buyers and perfect for those who dislike subtlety. It sells to arena suppliers, ogre-tale collectors, and anyone decorating a hall for intimidation.
flavorText: “Some clubs are not carried so much as negotiated with.”

name: shillelagh
shortDescription: A shillelagh of hard polished wood, simple at first glance and full of old roadside confidence. Travelers, shepherds, tavern keepers, and folk collectors all know its appeal: a walking stick that can end a discussion without admitting it came prepared.
flavorText: “A good shillelagh is polite until the owner is not.”

name: small club
shortDescription: A small club that fits easily in a pack, guard rack, or nervous shopkeeper’s drawer. It has little prestige, but it is cheap, durable, and direct, which makes it popular anywhere people need blunt answers without elaborate maintenance.
flavorText: “The small club is civilization’s least poetic backup plan.”

name: spiked club
shortDescription: A spiked club reinforced with metal teeth and a practical disregard for elegance. It is bought by caravan guards, rough militias, and collectors of intimidating wall pieces, though every sensible merchant stores it where sleeves cannot snag.
flavorText: “A club with spikes has abandoned debate.”

name: fabled spiked club of doom
shortDescription: The fabled spiked club of doom is an absurdly grim relic whose reputation is almost as heavy as its head. Doom cults, theatrical champions, and rich eccentrics all ask after it, each insisting their interest is completely different from the others.
flavorText: “Doom sells best when it is easy to lift and impossible to ignore.”

name: strong club
shortDescription: A strong club made from dense wood or reinforced core, built for repeated use without cracking, splintering, or becoming a sad stick. Guards, trainers, and frontier crews value its honest toughness, especially when metal weapons draw unwanted attention.
flavorText: “Strength does not always need an edge.”

name: weak club
shortDescription: A weak club with poor balance, light wood, or a history of being made too quickly for its own dignity. It is bought for practice yards, cheap guards, props, or desperate emergencies, and it never fails to make better weapons look worth the money.
flavorText: “A weak club teaches the value of every stronger one.”

name: arbalest
shortDescription: An arbalest with heavy limbs, crank-ready tension, and the stern patience of a weapon built to wait before striking. It appeals to fortress guards, siege buyers, and collectors who respect machinery that turns time into force.
flavorText: “An arbalest does not hurry. That is part of the threat.”
